coredata.dc:title: Multiwall-carbon nanotube modified by N-doped carbon quantum dots as Pt catalyst support for methanol electrooxidation
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jing-Jia
Wang, Zhen-Bo
Li, Chao
Zhao, Lei
Liu, Jing
Zhang, Li-Mei
Gu, Da-Ming
coredata.dc:description: The modification of N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) is an innovative approach to change the properties of multiwall-carbon nanotube (MWCNT). Here we report a facile hydrothermal treatment to synthesize NCQDs-MWCNT support, which acts as an improved catalyst support of Pt-based anode catalyst for direct methanol fuel cells. The structural properties of homemade catalysts are characterized by X-ray diffraction, Energy dispersive analysis of X-ray, transmission electron microscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The results indicate that Pt nanoparticles are well dispersed onto NCQDs-MWCNT and have a synergetic interaction with NCQDs. The results of electrochemical measurements reveal that Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT catalyst exhibits 1.3 times higher activity for methanol electrooxidation than that of Pt/MWCNT. The enhanced performance of Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT is attributed to the fact that NCQDs improve the dispersion of MWCNT and more uniform Pt nanoparticles are stabilized on NCQDs-MWCNT. NCQDs play a critical role in electrocatalytic performance for methanol electrooxidation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon quantum dots
A facile hydrothermal
Pt-based anode catalyst
Methanol electrooxidation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT and Pt/MWCNT catalysts
Physical characteristics of Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT and Pt/MWCNT catalysts
Electrochemical characteristics of Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT and Pt/MWCNT catalysts

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of NCQDs-MWCNT support, Pt/MWCNT and Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT catalysts.
EDX patterns of NCQDs-MWCNT support (a), Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT (b) and Pt/MWCNT catalysts (c).
TEM images of MWCNT (a) and HRTEM images of NCQDs-MWCNT (b).
TEM images of Pt/MWCNT (a) and Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT (b); HRTEM images of Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT (c).
XPS core level spectra of Pt/MWCNT (a) Pt 4f; XPS core level spectra of Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT (b) Pt 4f; (c) C 1s; (d) N 1s.

Electrochemical performance of Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT and Pt/MWCNT catalysts: cyclic voltammograms at the scan rate of 50 mV s−1 in 0.5 mol L−1 H2SO4 (a) and in methanol acidic medium (b), the inset is the linear sweep voltammetry at the scan rate of 0.5 mV s−1; Nyquist plots of EIS in methanol acidic medium (c), The inset of (c) is the equivalent circuit used for fitting the experimental data; amperometric i–t curves in methanol acidic medium (d).
Schematic of the Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT preparation process.
Results of the fits of the Pt 4f spectra.
Catalytic performance of Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT (a), PtIr/PW/MWCNT (b), Pt/PW/MWCNT (c), E-TEK (d) for methanol electrooxidation.

head.ce:title: Multiwall-carbon nanotube modified by N-doped carbon quantum dots as Pt catalyst support for methanol electrooxidation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Harbin Institute of Technology, No. 92 West-Da Zhi Street, Harbin, 150001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Heilongjiang University, No. 74 Xue-Fu Road, Harbin, 150080, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, Harbin Institute of Technology, No.92 West-Da Zhi Street, Harbin, 150001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The modification of N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) is an innovative approach to change the properties of multiwall-carbon nanotube (MWCNT). Here we report a facile hydrothermal treatment to synthesize NCQDs-MWCNT support, which acts as an improved catalyst support of Pt-based anode catalyst for direct methanol fuel cells. The structural properties of homemade catalysts are characterized by X-ray diffraction, Energy dispersive analysis of X-ray, transmission electron microscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The results indicate that Pt nanoparticles are well dispersed onto NCQDs-MWCNT and have a synergetic interaction with NCQDs. The results of electrochemical measurements reveal that Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT catalyst exhibits 1.3 times higher activity for methanol electrooxidation than that of Pt/MWCNT. The enhanced performance of Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT is attributed to the fact that NCQDs improve the dispersion of MWCNT and more uniform Pt nanoparticles are stabilized on NCQDs-MWCNT. NCQDs play a critical role in electrocatalytic performance for methanol electrooxidation.
The electrochemical performance of Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT catalyst is higher than that of Pt/MWCNT.

ce:section.ce:para: Direct methanol fuel cells (DMFCs) are promising power conversion devices for stationary and mobile applications in view of their high energy efficiency and environmental friendly [1–3]. Although the DMFCs have rapid development in recent decades, high cost of noble metal, poor methanol oxidation activity and short-life durability for catalysts are major issues for the wide commercialization of DMFCs [4,5]. These problems have become the critical challenges for successful introduction of DMFCs into the market. The catalyst support plays a significant role in the activity and durability of catalyst due to its structure, surface properties and interaction with the catalyst. Therefore, the exploration of advanced catalyst support is a promising approach to improve the electrocatalytic performance [6–8].

In general, the conventional carbon black (Vulcan XC-72) is the most widely used support for the dispersion of Pt nanoparticles. Nevertheless, Vulcan XC-72 corrosion leads to the decrease of catalytic activity during the long term working of DMFCs [9,10]. In order to enhance the catalyst stability, such as carbon nanotube (CNT) [11–13] and graphene [14,15] are widely researched. Owing to large surface area, high chemical stability and good electric conductivity, CNT has been an attractive catalyst support. However, CNT is chemically inert and generally do not provide enough surface groups for Pt deposition [16]. Thus, it needs to modify the surface of CNT for the sake of improving the dispersibility.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new form of nanostructured carbon, which have attracted much attention in recent years because of their chemical and physical properties. Heteroatom doping into CQDs has been becoming a more powerful way to improve the properties of CQDs and broaden their applications in analysis and electrocatalysis [17,18], such as N-doped CQDs (NCQDs) [19] and S-doped CQDs (SCQDs) [20], etc. The particle size of NCQDs is generally below 10 nm and its surface is functionalized by polar organic groups such as carboxylate, and/or hydroxyl groups and/or animo groups [21–23]. Furthermore, NCQDs can provide a promising way to tailor the transport properties of catalyst support for electronic applications [24]. The electronic structures of the host can be greatly modified by NCQDs, the oxygen-containing groups on NCQDs have been proposed to improve hydrophilicity in solution [25,26]. Therefore, NCQDs by virtue of their unique structural properties and good water-soluble have been applied to aspects of catalysis and electrochemistry [27].

Herein, in this work, we have designed an innovative support that multiwall-carbon nanotube (MWCNT) combined with NCQDs by one simple hydrothermal treatment and then synthesized Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT anode catalyst through microwave-assisted polyol process (MAPP). The results indicate that Pt catalyst supported on NCQDs-MWCNT presents the higher electrochemical activity for methanol electrooxidation. The effects of NCQDs on the catalytic performance of Pt catalyst are also investigated in detail.

Ethylenediamine tetraaceticacid disodium salt (EDTA) was purchased from Aladdin, Shanghai, China. MWCNT (with pristine specific surface areas is between 180 m2 g−1 and 200 m2 g−1 and diameter size is between 10 nm and 20 nm) was obtained from Chengdu Institute of Organic Chemistry, China. Hexachloroplatinic acid (H2PtCl6·6H2O) was purchased from Shanghai, China. 5 wt.% Nafion solution was purchased from Dupont. Except where specified, all chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received.

The NCQDs-MWCNT composite support was synthesized from the untreated MWCNT and EDTA by a facile one-step hydrothermal treatment. First, 20 mg of MWCNT and 3.0 g EDTA was dispersed in 30 mL of deionized water followed an ultrasonic treatment for 3 h. After that, the mixture was placed in a contraposition polyphenyl lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the slurry was taken out from the autoclave, washed with ultrapure water (Millipore, 18.2 MΩ cm) several times. The obtained NCQDs-MWCNT support was dried for 6 h at 80 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT and Pt/MWCNT catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT catalyst was synthesized by microwave-assisted ethylene glycol process (MAPP) [28]. To produce 20 wt.% of Pt loading catalyst, 20 mg NCQDs-MWCNT support was dispersed into the mixed solution of 60 mL containing ethylene glycol (EG) and isopropyl alcohol (v/v = 4:1) in a 150 mL beaker under ultrasonic treatment for 1 h to form uniform slurry. After the suspension was stirred for 3 h, and then adding a calculated amount of H2PtCl6-EG solution with agitation for 3 h. The pH value of mixture was adjusted to 12.0 with NaOH-EG (1 mol L−1) solution and suspension was heated for 55 s under the argon saturated by microwave oven. The HNO3 aqueous solution was added into mixture when the solution was cooled down to room temperature to adjust pH value of the solution to about 2–3. The suspension was stirred for 12 h by mechanical agitation and the product was washed several times with deionized water and dried under vacuum at 80 °C for 5 h. The 20 wt.% Pt/MWCNT catalyst was synthesized by the same procedures for preparing the Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT.

Crystal structure in catalyst was obtained by XRD analysis with the D/max-RB diffractometer (made in Japan) using a Cu Ka X-ray source operating at 45 kV and 100 mA, scanning between 10° and 90° at a rate of 40° min−1.
TEM images of all samples were characterized by using a TECNAI G2F30 field emission transmission electron microscope with a spatial resolution of 0.17 nm.
The UV–vis absorption spectra of NCQDs were recorded by a Perkin–Elmer Lambda 20 spectrometer with 1 × 1 cm quartz cuvette along the 1 cm length.
Fluorescence experiments of NCQDs were performed by a Hitachi FL-4500 fluorescence spectrometer.

XPS measurements were carried out on PHI model 5700 instrument from Physical Electronics Corporation to determine the surface properties of the catalysts. The Al Kα X-ray source was operated at 250 W and the take-off angle of the sample to analyzer was 45°. Survey spectra were collected at a pass energy (PE) of 187.85 eV over a binding energy range from 0 eV to 1300 eV. High binding energy resolution multiplex data for the individual elements were collected at a PE of 29.55 eV. During all XPS experiments, the pressure inside the vacuum system was maintained at 1 × 10−9 Pa.
All energy dispersive X-ray detector spectrum (EDX) measurements were performed with a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi Ltd. S-4700).

Electrochemical measurements were performed with CHI 650E electrochemical analysis instrument. The cells used were conventional three-electrode electrochemical cells. The saturated mercury sulfate electrode (MSE, Hg/Hg2SO4, all of the potentials reported are relative to the MSE electrode) was used as the reference electrode. A platinum network was used as a counter electrode [29]. The glassy carbon working electrode with 4 mm of diameter was used as a working electrode. working electrode was prepared as follows: The catalyst ink was prepared by ultrasonically dispersing a mixture including 2.0 mg of catalyst, 2.0 mL compound of ethanol and ultrapure water (V/V = 1:1) for 20 min. Next, 10 μL of this ink was transferred onto a pre-cleaned glassy carbon disk, and onto which 5 μL of a 5 wt.% Nafion® solution. Cyclic voltammograms (CV) measurements were carried out in reference to MSE in 0.5 mol L−1 H2SO4 solution purged with high-purity argon gas (Ar). The electrochemical oxidation of methanol on the catalyst was measured in an Ar-saturated solution of 0.5 mol L−1 H2SO4 containing 0.5 mol L−1 CH3OH. The electrochemical active surface area (ESA) of platinum was calculated with the formula ESAPt = QH/(0.21M Pt) [30]. QH is the charge due to the hydrogen adsorption/desorption in the hydrogen region of the CVs. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was recorded at frequencies between 100 kHz and 0.01Hz with 12 points per decade. The amplitude of the sinusoidal potential signal was 5 mV. The amperometric i–t curve was obtained at a constant potential of 0.65 V for 3600 s in a methanol acidic medium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical characteristics of Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT and Pt/MWCNT catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis process of Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT catalyst has been described in detail in Scheme 1 . It shows three steps for preparation as follows: (1) one-step hydrothermal treatment of the untreated MWCNT and EDTA, (2) the combination of NCQDs and MWCNT for generating the NCQDs-MWCNT support, (3) the deposition of Pt nanoparticles onto NCQDs-MWCNT support. Meanwhile, the production of NCQDs from EDTA through three stages: decomposition, polymerization, and carbonization. EDTA decomposes to remove one or more CH3COOH groups [31]. After that, water evaporation at high temperature spurs the intermolecular dehydration of intermediates. Crosslinked polymerization then occurs via the intermolecular amide linkage between –NH and –COOH groups to form “polymer-like” intermediates [32,33]. These intermediates are finally carbonized and simultaneously capped to form the water-soluble NCQDs [34]. Through the method of hydrothermal treatment, NCQDs closely combine with MWCNT via π–π bonding [35], which effects dispersion of MWCNT in solution. In addition, Pt nanoparticles are designed to deposit on NCQDs-MWCNT support more uniformly, when the oxygen-containing groups on NCQDs have been proposed to provide more deposition sites for Pt nanoparticles. The existence of NCQDs in the NCQDs-MWCNT can enhance the interaction between Pt nanoparticles and support material, which can further improve electrochemical performance of Pt-based catalyst [36].

The synthesis of NCQDs-MWCNT, Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT and Pt/MWCNT is described in the experimental section. The NCQDs-MWCNT, Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT and Pt/MWCNT are characterized by XRD as shown in Fig. 1 . In the XRD pattern, representative reflection peak of Pt (111) is distinctly observed in Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT and Pt/MWCNT spectra, which implies that Pt forms the face-centered cubic (fcc) crystal structure. Because the face-centered cubic (fcc) phase is only structure for platinum, the Pt metal is subsistent in the form of a face-centered cubic (fcc) phase. The 2θ values of the (1 1 1) peak for Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT and Pt/MWCNT were 39.5° and 39.0°, respectively. Moreover, the broader diffraction peaks for catalysts also led to smaller average Pt nanoparticle size [37], which are in good shown with TEM images (Fig. 4). The presence of a small broader heave (see the inset in Fig. 1) is corresponding to amorphous N-doped carbon from 2θ = 20.6° to 22.2° [38].

Besides, as shown in Fig. 2 , the Pt mass contents of Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT and Pt/MWCNT catalysts determined by EDX analysis are 21.6 wt.% and 20.7 wt.%, respectively. Because of the production processes are the same and the yields of both catalysts are very close, it can be confirmed that the Pt loadings of both catalysts are similar to the theoretical value of 20 wt.%.

HRTEM images of untreated MWCNT and NCQDs-MWCNT are shown in Fig. 3 . It is clearly seen that the bare MWCNT are modified by the uniform of NCQDs (Fig. 3b and Fig. S1). Corresponding to this result, it can be concluded that NCQDs combine with MWCNT via π–π bonding through the method of hydrothermal treatment. Furthermore, the UV–vis and fluorescence test show that the characteristic of NCQDs (Fig. S2). Hence, NCQDs are proved to the identification of existence.

TEM images of Pt/MWCNT and Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT catalysts are shown in Fig. 4 . From the TEM and HRTEM images as shown in Fig. 4, after the deposition of Pt on NCQDs-MWCNT, the Pt nanoparticles are uniformly distributed over the whole surface and many Pt nanoparticles reside closely to NCQDs on MWCNT (Fig. 4c). At the same time, the dispersion of Pt nanoparticles on NCQDs-MWCNT is more uniform than that of Pt nanoparticles on MWCNT (Fig. 4b).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is an efficient method to analyze chemical state information of elements. Fig. 5 shows the binding energies of N1S, C1s and Pt4f peak spectra for Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT and Pt4f peak spectra for Pt/MWCNT. The binding energies of Pt4f along with their relative intensities are provided in Table 1 . Not surprisingly, the Pt(0) peaks of Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT catalyst show a shift of the Pt 4f binding energy to the direction of higher energies by about 0.29 eV in comparison with that of Pt/MWCNT (see Fig. 5b). The possible reason is that functional groups of NCQDs on support surface can intensify the electron withdrawing effect against Pt and strengthen the metal-support interaction between Pt nanoparticles and the support [39]. Moreover, the content of Pt(0) increases by 11% accompanied with decreasing by 9.83% of Pt(IV), demonstrating that Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT catalyst has higher durability relative to Pt/MWCNT due to more corrosion resistance of Pt(0) [40].

In addition, the photoelectron spectra of the C1s and N1s need to further identify carbon and nitrogen bonding configuration in the Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT catalyst. The main peak at 284.53 eV in high-resolution C1s spectra in Fig. 5c corresponds to CC, demonstrating that most of the C atoms are arranged in a conjugated honeycomb lattice. The small peaks at 285.70 eV and 287.10 eV reflect different bonding structures of the C–N bonds, corresponding to the N-sp2C and N-sp3C bonds, respectively, originating from substitution of the N atoms or defects [41]. The N1s peak (see Fig. 5d) of the Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT consists of three components concentrated on 398.40 eV, 400.10 eV and 402.60 eV, which can be attributed to pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N and graphitic-N, respectively. It has been pointed out that the terms pyridinic and pyrrolic are referred to the N atoms, which are located in a π conjugated system and contribute to the π system with one or two p-electrons, respectively [42]. Both of them can effectively strengthen the electron-transfer of Pt nanoparticles to NCQDs-MWCNT [43], leading to the stronger metal-support interactions between Pt nanoparticles and the NCQDs-MWCNT support.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characteristics of Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT and Pt/MWCNT catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: CV curves for Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT and Pt/MWCNT catalysts are carried out in an Ar-saturated solution of 0.5 mol L−1 H2SO4 at 25 °C and shown in Fig. 6 a. The electrochemically active specific surface area (ESA) of Pt nanoparticles is obtained by the measurements of the hydrogen adsorption-desorption (HAD) integrals. The variation of ESAPt can be obviously seen with different supports. ESAPt of Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT and Pt/MWCNT catalysts are 46.5 m2 g−1 and 36.4 m2 g−1, respectively. The result illustrates that the ESAPt of Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT catalyst is larger than that of Pt/MWCNT. Contacted with the previously physical characterization mentioned, it can be demonstrated that the ESAPt of catalyst is largely interrelated to the composition of support. Pt nanoparticles are much easier to deposit on NCQDs-MWCNT owing to π–π interaction combining between NCQDs and MWCNT [35]. It can not only improve dispersion for MWCNT, but also intensify distribution of different active sites served for Pt nanoparticles deposition.

The activity of two catalysts for methanol electrooxidation is measured by CV measurement in an Ar-saturated solution of 0.5 mol L−1 H2SO4 containing 0.5 mol L−1 CH3OH at 25 °C. Fig. 6b shows the CV curves for Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT and Pt/MWCNT catalysts. The forward peak current density is generally regarded as methanol electrooxidation on non-poisoned catalyst. Thus, the electrochemical activity could be described by the forward peak current density of catalyst [44]. The forward peak current densities of Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT and Pt/MWCNT catalysts are about 0.42 A mg−1 and 0.33 A mg−1, respectively. The eletrocatalytic activity of Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT is 1.3 times higher than that of Pt/MWCNT catalyst, demonstrating that the electrooxidation activity of Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT has a moderate increase. Meanwhile, it is found that the value of Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT is higher than PtIr/PW/MWCNT (0.28 A mg−1), Pt/PW/MWCNT (0.094 A mg−1) [45] and E-TEK (0.26 A mg−1) [46] for methanol electrooxidation in Table 2 . Moreover, Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT exhibits the lower onset potential for methanol electrooxidation (inset of Fig. 6b), indicating that the Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT catalyst shows a relatively good electrocatalytic activity. In addition, the ratio of the forward oxidation current peak (If) to the reverse current peak (Ib), If/Ib, can be used to infer the CO tolerance of the catalyst. The If/Ib ratio of the Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT catalyst is 0.86, which is higher than that of Pt/MWCNT (If/Ib = 0.76). The results suggesting that the methanol molecules can be more effectively oxidized on the Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT catalyst during the forward scan, generating relatively fewer poisoning species compared to Pt/MWCNT, indicating a better CO tolerance.

Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) is used to determine charge transfer resistance, which could reflect the methanol electrooxidation activity of various catalysts. The Nyquist plots of EIS spectra for Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT and Pt/MWCNT are shown in Fig. 6c. The semicircle diameter is correlated with charge transfer resistance. The width of the semicircle is proportional to the charge-transfer resistance of the catalyst material, the smaller the diameter of the semicircle is, and the better the catalytic activity is. It can be obtained from Fig. 6c, the semicircle of Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT catalyst is smaller than Pt/MWCNT catalyst, suggesting that its methanol electrooxidation activity is much better. The equivalent circuit is used to fit the EIS data and is shown in inset of Fig. 6c. Here R1 and R2 are the solution resistance and the charge-transfer resistance, respectively. CPE1 is the electric double-layer capacitance used instead of a capacitor to account for the porous catalysts. R3 and L1 are the resistance and inductance of the adsorbed layer [47]. According to stimulated parameters, the charge-transfer resistance (Rct) for Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT (87.6 Ω cm2) is much lower than that of Pt/MWCNT (129.4 Ω cm2). EIS provide additional evidence that Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT possesses better activity towards methanol electrooxidation.

The amperometric i–t curves of Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT and Pt/MWCNT catalysts are obtained at a constant potential of 0.6 V for 3600 s in a methanol acidic medium (0.5 mol L−1 H2SO4 + 0.5 mol L−1 CH3OH). It can be seen from Fig. 6d that the final current density on Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT after 3600 s is 9.3 mA mg−1, higher than that of Pt/MWCNT (4.6 mA mg−1). It is well known that the amperometric i–t curves can simultaneously reflect the activity and stability of catalysts. The potentiostatic current decreases rapidly at the initial stage, which might be due to the formation of intermediate species, such as COads and CHOads etc., during methanol electrooxidation reaction [48]. It appears that the current density on Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT is much higher than that on Pt/MWCNT catalyst during the whole testing time, indicating that the electrocatalytic activity of Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT for methanol electrooxidation is higher than that of Pt/MWCNT catalyst.

Overall, the Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT catalyst shows better electrochemical performance, compared with the Pt/MWCNT. The improvements of activity for the Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT catalyst can be ascribed to the synergetic effect between Pt and NCQDs, the stronger hydrophilicity of NCQD-MWCNT compared with bare MWCNT and the better dispersion of Pt nanoparticles on NCQD-MWCNT support.

The novel NCQDs-MWCNT support has been successfully prepared by a facile hydrothermal synthesis method for the first time in this paper. The unique NCQDs significantly promote the dispersion for MWCNT when they combine with MWCNT via π–π bonding. Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT catalyst exhibits better electrochemical activity compared with Pt/MWCNT. Owing to a strong metal-support interaction between Pt nanoparticles and the composite support, Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT catalyst shows the higher electrocatalytic performance. Moreover, the discovery of inexpensive and easily obtainable NCQDs can act as a new promoter of MWCNT in DMFC is a significant advance. This research raises the enormous possibility of using NCQDs as a highly efficient catalyst promoter for methanol electrooxidation process.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21273058), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grant No.2012M520731 and 2014T70350), Heilongjiang postdoctoral financial assistance (LBH-Z12089) for their financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jpowsour.2015.04.150.
